q: A financial services company needs to design a multi-region VPC architecture. What CIDR strategy enables non-conflicting connectivity?
options: Use the same CIDR block in both regions
Use overlapping CIDR blocks with NAT instances
Use non-overlapping CIDR blocks and enable Transit Gateway peering
Use secondary CIDR blocks on all VPCs
explain: Non-overlapping CIDR blocks are a prerequisite for any AWS inter-VPC routing because AWS cannot route between VPCs with overlapping address spaces — not even with NAT. Transit Gateway peering between regions then provides the routing plane that connects those non-overlapping VPCs without requiring a full mesh of individual peering connections. Using the same CIDR in both regions (option A) is the most common cause of routing failures in multi-region designs.

q: An enterprise needs hub-and-spoke topology across 12 AWS accounts. Which service provides centralized routing?
options: AWS App Mesh
VPC Peering with a central hub account
AWS VPN CloudHub
AWS Transit Gateway
explain: AWS Transit Gateway is purpose-built for hub-and-spoke architectures, supporting up to 5,000 attachments (VPCs, VPNs, Direct Connect gateways) with a single centralized route table per attachment. VPC Peering (option B) requires n*(n-1)/2 peering connections — 66 connections for 12 accounts — and is non-transitive, meaning spoke-to-spoke traffic cannot traverse a hub VPC. Transit Gateway solves both problems with one attachment per spoke.

q: A company requires IPv6 support for EC2 instances in a VPC. What must be configured for full dual-stack functionality?
options: Assign IPv6 CIDR block to VPC only
Assign IPv6 CIDR block to VPC and subnets, then add IPv6 route table entries
Only assign IPv6 addresses to instances
Use NAT64 translation only
explain: IPv6 enablement in a VPC is a three-step process: assign an Amazon-provided /56 IPv6 CIDR to the VPC, assign a /64 IPv6 CIDR to each subnet that needs IPv6, and add route table entries pointing ::/0 to an Internet Gateway (for public subnets) or an Egress-Only Internet Gateway (for private subnets). Assigning a CIDR to the VPC alone (option A) does not make any subnet or instance reachable via IPv6.

q: A healthcare provider needs redundant hybrid connectivity with consistent performance. What should be the primary design?
options: Single VPN connection with Direct Connect as backup
Multiple VPNs on different internet endpoints
Single Direct Connect connection with VPN failover
Multiple Direct Connect connections across diverse locations and carriers
explain: Multiple Direct Connect connections at geographically separate AWS Direct Connect locations using different carriers provides true redundancy: no single point of failure at the fiber, facility, or carrier level. A single Direct Connect with VPN failover (option C) introduces internet variability during failover and violates the consistent-performance requirement. For healthcare workloads, consistent sub-millisecond latency and no internet traversal are non-negotiable.

q: An organization must implement AWS PrivateLink for internal service sharing across VPCs. Which endpoint type enables this?
options: VPC peering with security groups
Gateway endpoint backed by ALB
Gateway endpoint
Interface endpoint with Network Load Balancer
explain: AWS PrivateLink uses Interface VPC Endpoints, which create Elastic Network Interfaces (ENIs) with private IP addresses in the consumer VPC. The producer side must front the service with a Network Load Balancer, which is the only load balancer type supported as a PrivateLink service endpoint. Gateway endpoints (option C) are only available for S3 and DynamoDB and do not support PrivateLink service sharing.

q: A company needs network segmentation with a DMZ architecture for a three-tier web application. Which combination best implements this?
options: Public, private, and protected subnets with separate route tables per tier
Only private subnets with VPN endpoint
Only public subnets with security groups
Public and private subnets with NAT Gateway only
explain: A proper DMZ requires three distinct network tiers: a public subnet (web/DMZ) with routes to the Internet Gateway, a private subnet (application) routing outbound through a NAT Gateway, and a protected subnet (database) with no internet route at all. Each tier must have its own route table to enforce these distinct egress policies — sharing a single route table across tiers defeats the purpose of DMZ segmentation.

q: A multi-tenant SaaS platform requires complete customer VPC isolation. What prevents IP conflicts while enabling controlled communication?
options: Multiple VPCs with Transit Gateway and custom route tables
Single VPC with overlapping CIDR blocks and NAT
Shared VPC with separate subnets
VPC peering between all customer VPCs
explain: Separate VPCs per customer with non-overlapping CIDRs connected via Transit Gateway provides complete network isolation at the VPC boundary. Custom Transit Gateway route tables allow selective routing — for example, allowing each customer VPC to reach shared services but not each other. Overlapping CIDRs (option B) make routing impossible without complex NAT translation, and a shared VPC (option C) provides no network-layer isolation between tenants.

q: A company needs routing between on-premises locations, multiple AWS regions, and multiple AWS accounts from a single management plane. Which service enables this?
options: AWS Cloud WAN
AWS Wavelength
AWS Global Accelerator
Route 53 health checks
explain: AWS Cloud WAN provides a centralized global network management console with a policy-based routing engine that spans on-premises locations (via Direct Connect or VPN), multiple AWS regions, and multiple AWS accounts. It automates the creation and management of Transit Gateways and their attachments. Global Accelerator (option C) optimizes application traffic routing but does not provide network-layer routing between on-premises, regions, and accounts.

q: An organization requires DNS resolution from on-premises systems to AWS private hosted zones. What is the recommended architecture?
options: Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoints with conditional forwarding rules on-premises
Route 53 public hosted zone with on-premises DNS forwarding
Route 53 private hosted zone with no resolver configuration
On-premises DNS as the primary authority for all zones
explain: Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoints create ENIs in specified subnets that accept DNS queries forwarded from on-premises resolvers. The on-premises DNS server is then configured with a conditional forwarding rule directing queries for the AWS domain (e.g., internal.example.com) to those inbound endpoint IPs. A public hosted zone (option B) would expose internal DNS records to the internet, violating the private connectivity requirement.

q: A financial institution requires multiple transit routes and flexible path selection across VPCs. What Transit Gateway feature enables this?
options: Transit Gateway multicast
Transit Gateway route tables
Transit Gateway NAT
Transit Gateway peering
explain: Transit Gateway route tables are the routing control plane for all TGW traffic. Each attachment can be associated with a specific route table, and route tables can contain static routes or accept propagated routes from attachments. This enables sophisticated topologies such as isolated route tables per environment (production vs. dev), shared services routing, and blackhole routes for security. Multicast (option A) is for one-to-many delivery, not path selection.

q: A company must quickly design a VPC for 50 instances across 2 AZs with room for future growth. What minimum subnet architecture ensures flexibility?
options: One public, one private, and one protected subnet per AZ
One public and one private subnet per AZ
Design based only on current needs
One subnet per AZ
explain: Three-tier subnet design (public/web, private/application, protected/database) per AZ provides immediate workload isolation at the network layer and accommodates future application tiers without redesign. Starting with only two tiers (option B) forces a redesign when database isolation becomes a compliance requirement. AWS best practice recommends planning subnet tiers based on isolation requirements rather than current instance count.

q: A healthcare organization must meet HIPAA compliance requirements. Which VPC architecture satisfies the network isolation requirements?
options: Subnets with NAT Gateway for anonymization
Public subnet with encryption at rest only
VPC peering with external providers
Isolated private subnets with no direct internet access and strict egress controls
explain: HIPAA requires that PHI (Protected Health Information) systems have no direct internet exposure. This means private subnets with no public IP addresses, NAT Gateway for controlled outbound access, Security Groups restricting inbound to known sources, and Network ACLs as a second layer of defense. A public subnet with encryption (option B) still exposes the instance directly to the internet, which fails the network isolation requirement.

q: A startup requires low-latency on-premises to AWS connectivity with a limited budget. What is the most cost-effective option?
options: Dedicated Direct Connect circuit with failover
Direct Connect with dedicated connection
Multiple redundant Direct Connect connections
Site-to-Site VPN over internet
explain: AWS Site-to-Site VPN costs approximately $0.05/hour per tunnel plus data transfer, requiring no physical port fees or colocation costs. Direct Connect dedicated connections (option B) require physical cross-connect fees, colocation, and port fees starting at several hundred dollars per month before data transfer charges. For a startup's non-production workloads or limited budget, VPN provides adequate connectivity at a fraction of the cost.

q: A financial institution requires sub-millisecond latency for high-frequency trading between its data center and AWS. Which connectivity option best guarantees this?
options: VPC Interface endpoints
Direct Connect with colocation at an AWS Direct Connect location
Site-to-Site VPN
CloudFront edge locations
explain: AWS Direct Connect with colocation physically places the customer's routing equipment in the same facility as the AWS Direct Connect router, minimizing the cable distance to single-digit milliseconds or sub-millisecond round trips. This dedicated fiber path bypasses the public internet entirely and avoids VPN encryption overhead. Site-to-Site VPN (option C) traverses the internet with variable latency due to BGP path selection and shared congestion, making it unsuitable for latency-sensitive trading.

q: A company needs transit connectivity between multiple on-premises locations using AWS as the network backbone. What pattern accomplishes this?
options: Transit Gateway with multiple VPN attachments
VPC peering between on-premises office VPNs
Site-to-Site VPN hub at a central on-premises location
Separate Direct Connect connections to each office
explain: AWS Transit Gateway enables on-premises-to-on-premises communication through AWS by connecting each office via a Site-to-Site VPN attachment. When VPN route propagation is enabled, the Transit Gateway route table learns all on-premises CIDRs and routes traffic between them via the AWS backbone. This is functionally identical to AWS VPN CloudHub but with better scalability and management through Transit Gateway's centralized route tables.

q: A company needs to expand its VPC's IP address space when the primary CIDR block is nearly exhausted, without disrupting running instances. What approach should be used?
options: Migrate all instances to a new larger VPC
Request AWS to expand the primary CIDR block
Split instances across multiple new VPCs
Add secondary CIDR blocks to the existing VPC
explain: AWS supports adding up to 4 secondary IPv4 CIDR blocks to an existing VPC (5 total including primary), with supported ranges from RFC 1918 space. Secondary CIDRs are immediately available for new subnet creation without touching existing subnets, instances, route tables, or security groups. Migrating to a new VPC (option A) would require re-creating all networking resources and updating application configurations, causing significant downtime.

q: A SaaS provider requires complete multi-tenant architecture with per-customer network isolation. What design achieves this?
options: Overlapping CIDR blocks with NAT translation
Separate VPC per customer with Transit Gateway for shared services
Shared VPC with customer-specific subnets
Single VPC with per-customer security groups
explain: Separate VPCs per customer provide VPC-boundary isolation — the strongest network isolation available in AWS. Transit Gateway connects each customer VPC to a shared services VPC (for logging, DNS, etc.) while Transit Gateway route tables can be configured to prevent any customer VPC from reaching other customer VPCs. A shared VPC with subnets (option C) provides only subnet-level isolation, which can be bypassed by misconfigured security groups.

q: A company requires redundant Direct Connect connectivity across multiple carriers. What is the minimum configuration for true carrier redundancy?
options: Multiple Direct Connect connections at diverse AWS locations using different carriers
Single connection with Link Aggregation Group (LAG) at the same location
VPN backup on a separate internet connection
Single connection with LAG
explain: True carrier redundancy requires Direct Connect connections that do not share any physical infrastructure: different AWS Direct Connect locations (different facilities) and different carriers (different fiber paths). A LAG at the same location (option B) protects against individual port failure but shares the same physical facility and carrier — a facility outage or carrier BGP issue takes down all LAG members simultaneously.

q: A company must isolate production and non-production workloads at the network layer. Which design provides the strongest isolation?
options: Separate subnets within the same VPC
Security groups only
Separate VPCs with Transit Gateway and selective routing
IAM policies only
explain: VPC-boundary isolation is stronger than subnet-level isolation because the VPC is a logically isolated network boundary in AWS. Separate VPCs for production and non-production prevent any accidental routing between environments. Transit Gateway with selective route tables then allows controlled, auditable communication (e.g., non-prod can reach a shared CI/CD VPC but not production). Security groups alone (option B) provide instance-level filtering but within the same VPC, reducing defense-in-depth.

q: A company plans a VPC that must scale from 1,000 to 100,000 IP addresses. What CIDR block is appropriate?
explain: A /8 CIDR block provides approximately 16.7 million IP addresses, which is the only option that can accommodate 100,000 instances after accounting for AWS's 5 reserved addresses per subnet and subnetting overhead across multiple tiers and AZs. A /16 (option D) provides only 65,536 addresses — after subnetting overhead, this typically accommodates 30,000–40,000 instances, insufficient for 100,000. Planning the CIDR at build-time avoids the painful process of adding secondary CIDRs later.

q: A multi-account organization needs centralized egress inspection through a Security Hub VPC. What architecture enables this?
options: Transit Gateway hub-and-spoke with central egress VPC containing NAT Gateway
NAT Gateway in each spoke VPC
Direct Connect to the security hub
VPC peering to the security hub
explain: The centralized egress pattern uses Transit Gateway to route all spoke VPC outbound traffic to a single security/egress VPC containing NAT Gateway, Network Firewall, and inspection appliances. Spoke VPCs have a default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to the Transit Gateway, and the Transit Gateway route table directs that traffic to the egress VPC. Each spoke having its own NAT Gateway (option B) makes centralized inspection impossible and increases costs.

q: A company requires a gradual on-premises to cloud migration strategy. What hybrid architecture supports incremental traffic shifting?
options: VPN only for security during migration
Direct Connect with BGP weighted routing for load sharing between on-premises and cloud
Cloud-only network from day one
Completely separate on-premises and cloud networks
explain: Direct Connect with BGP weighted routing allows gradual traffic percentage shifts by adjusting BGP local preference or MED values. For example, initially routing 10% of traffic to AWS while 90% remains on-premises, then gradually inverting the ratio as confidence grows. This provides a controlled migration path without a hard cutover. VPN-only (option A) works but lacks the bandwidth and consistency needed for production traffic shifts in most enterprise environments.

q: A global company needs multiple independent regional data centers in AWS. What topology provides regional independence with selective inter-region communication?
options: No interconnection between regions
Single global VPC
Full mesh VPC peering between all regions
Regional Transit Gateways with selective inter-region peering
explain: Regional Transit Gateways provide independent routing domains per region, and AWS Transit Gateway inter-region peering allows selective connectivity between specific regions. Each regional TGW route table can be configured to accept only the routes needed for authorized inter-region communication. Full mesh VPC peering (option C) creates n*(n-1)/2 peering connections and cannot enforce transitive routing policies between regions.

q: A company with 5 offices needs cost-effective connectivity to AWS with centralized routing management. What is the most cost-effective architecture?
options: Site-to-Site VPN from each office attached to a Transit Gateway
Dedicated Direct Connect from each office
Physical LANs between each office
Direct Connect from the central office only
explain: Site-to-Site VPN costs approximately $0.05/hour per tunnel versus Direct Connect dedicated connections at several hundred dollars per month per location. Attaching all 5 VPNs to a Transit Gateway (one TGW instead of 5 Virtual Private Gateways) centralizes routing and enables office-to-office communication via the AWS backbone. Direct Connect from each office (option B) provides better performance but at 10–20x the cost.

q: A startup requires rapid VPC design for 50 instances across 2 AZs. What minimum subnet architecture ensures long-term flexibility?
options: One public and one private subnet per AZ
One public, one private, and one protected subnet per AZ
Design subnets based on growth projections only
One subnet per AZ
explain: Three-tier subnet architecture (public for load balancers, private for application servers, protected for databases) per AZ is the AWS Well-Architected Framework recommendation. Starting with two tiers (option A) typically requires a subnet restructuring when database isolation becomes a compliance requirement, which causes downtime during migration. AWS reserves 5 IP addresses per subnet, making it important to size subnets generously from the start.

q: An organization must meet GDPR data residency requirements. What VPC design enforces geographic data boundaries?
options: Shared VPC across regions with regional subnets
CloudFront with regional cache configurations
Separate VPCs deployed only in compliant AWS regions
Single global VPC
explain: AWS VPCs are regional resources — all resources in a VPC physically reside in that region. Creating VPCs only in GDPR-compliant EU regions (e.g., eu-west-1, eu-central-1) and explicitly not creating VPCs in non-EU regions ensures that data stored or processed in those VPCs never physically leaves the EU. A 'global VPC' does not exist in AWS — all VPCs are regional.

q: A company implements Transit Gateway instead of VPC peering. What is the primary advantage over VPC peering?
options: Lower cost per GB of data transferred
Eliminates the need for route tables
Transit Gateway provides lower latency than VPC peering
One Transit Gateway attachment per VPC replaces the full mesh of peering connections
explain: VPC peering requires n*(n-1)/2 connections for full mesh — 10 VPCs require 45 peering connections. Transit Gateway replaces all of those with a single attachment per VPC, reducing management complexity from O(n²) to O(n). Transit Gateway actually adds slight latency compared to direct VPC peering (which is direct routing), and Transit Gateway data processing costs are typically higher than VPC peering, making options A and C incorrect.

q: A company requires high availability while minimizing inter-AZ data transfer costs. What architecture achieves this balance?
options: Sticky sessions within AZ with ALB zone affinity
Single AZ deployment
Compute distributed across AZs with minimal cross-AZ data replication
Route all traffic through a single AZ
explain: AWS charges $0.01/GB for inter-AZ data transfer. Distributing compute across AZs provides fault tolerance, and designing applications to minimize cross-AZ calls (e.g., stateless tiers, local caching) reduces costs. Single AZ (option B) eliminates cross-AZ costs but sacrifices availability entirely — AWS recommends multi-AZ for production workloads. Sticky sessions (option A) only route client traffic locally, not backend service calls.

q: A financial services firm requires a three-tier VPC architecture spanning 2 AZs. What is the minimum number of route tables?
options: One route table for all subnets
Determined by compliance requirements only
One route table per tier per AZ
One route table per tier (3 total)
explain: Each tier in each AZ requires its own route table to enforce distinct egress policies: web tier routes to Internet Gateway, app tier routes to NAT Gateway in the same AZ, and database tier has no internet route. Sharing a route table across AZs for the same tier means both AZs use the same NAT Gateway — creating a single point of failure for outbound connectivity. With 3 tiers × 2 AZs, the minimum is 6 route tables plus 1 for the public subnet.

q: A company designs VPC endpoints for S3 access to optimize cost. Which endpoint type is preferred for cost optimization?
options: Gateway endpoint
Interface endpoint always
Interface endpoint with NLB
Both are equally cost-effective
explain: Gateway VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB have no hourly charge and no per-GB data processing fee — they are completely free. Traffic through a Gateway endpoint also bypasses NAT Gateway, eliminating NAT Gateway data processing costs ($0.045/GB). Interface endpoints create ENIs and charge $0.01/hour plus $0.01/GB processed, making them more expensive for high-volume S3 workloads. Use Interface endpoints for S3 only when private DNS or cross-region requirements demand it.

q: A startup must quickly scale its VPC from 1,000 to 10,000 instances. What CIDR strategy enables this without redesign?
options: Plan primary and secondary CIDR ranges hierarchically from the start
Add a secondary CIDR when the primary runs out
Use a minimal /28 CIDR initially
Create a new VPC each time capacity is exhausted
explain: Hierarchical CIDR planning allocates the primary CIDR generously and pre-defines secondary CIDR ranges before they are needed. This ensures that when capacity expands, the secondary ranges are already documented, non-overlapping with other VPCs, and within the RFC 1918 space. Reactive secondary CIDR addition (option B) risks choosing ranges that conflict with VPC peering or Transit Gateway routes already in use, causing routing failures.

q: A company implementing AWS Client VPN needs clients to access both AWS VPC resources and on-premises resources through the VPN. What configuration is required?
options: VPN endpoint, hybrid connectivity (Direct Connect or Site-to-Site VPN), and authorization rules for all CIDRs
Client configuration file with on-premises routes only
VPN endpoint only
Direct tunneling to on-premises
explain: Reaching on-premises resources through Client VPN requires three components: (1) the Client VPN endpoint associated with a VPC subnet, (2) hybrid connectivity (Direct Connect or Site-to-Site VPN) between that VPC and on-premises, and (3) authorization rules granting client IP range access to on-premises CIDR blocks. Without the authorization rules, clients are denied access even if the network path exists.

q: A company with offices in 5 cities needs simple global connectivity to AWS. Which design minimizes management complexity?
options: Site-to-Site VPN from each office attached to a Transit Gateway hub
On-premises VPN hub at the central office
Physical dedicated LANs between each city
Separate Direct Connect from each office
explain: AWS Transit Gateway with VPN attachments from each office eliminates the need for an on-premises hub appliance and provides built-in redundancy via dual VPN tunnels per attachment. The TGW acts as the central hub with a managed route table, replacing the complex BGP configuration required on an on-premises hub router. An on-premises VPN hub (option B) creates a single point of failure and requires the customer to manage high-availability appliances.

q: A company requires disaster recovery with sub-minute RTO across regions. What architecture achieves this?
options: Single region standby with hourly backups
Active-active across regions with real-time data replication
Periodic snapshot replication across regions
Active-passive with manual failover
explain: Sub-minute RTO requires both applications and data to be immediately available without any restoration step. Active-active with real-time bi-directional replication (using DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database, or Kinesis cross-region replication) means failover is simply a DNS update or traffic policy change, completing in seconds. Active-passive with any form of restore step (option D) inherently requires time to detect failure, initiate restore, and verify completion — typically 5–30 minutes.

q: A company plans a VPC needing to support 256 distinct subnets for a complex organizational structure. What minimum VPC CIDR is needed?
explain: A /16 CIDR (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16) provides 65,536 addresses and can be divided into exactly 256 subnets of /24 size (each with 251 usable IPs after AWS reserves 5). This is the minimum CIDR that can accommodate 256 meaningful subnets. A /20 (option C) provides only 4,096 addresses — enough for 16 subnets of /24, far short of 256. A /12 would work but wastes address space unnecessarily.

q: A company requires network design supporting per-facility customer segmentation with strict egress controls and audit logging. What minimum design achieves this?
options: Shared subnets across facilities with shared routing
Isolated private subnets per facility with restricted egress and VPC Flow Logs enabled
Public VPC with TLS encryption only
Private subnets with unrestricted internet access
explain: Per-facility isolation requires separate subnet boundaries (not just security groups) so that NACLs can enforce subnet-level access controls independently per facility. Restricted egress prevents data exfiltration, and VPC Flow Logs provide the audit trail required for compliance. Shared subnets (option A) cannot provide independent NACLs per facility because NACLs are applied at the subnet level, not the resource level.

q: An organization needs to expose a VPC endpoint service to external customers. What must be configured on both the producer and consumer sides?
options: Producer creates PrivateLink service endpoint; consumer creates an interface endpoint to connect
Producer creates service endpoint in the provider account only
Consumer creates an interface endpoint in their VPC only
VPC peering between producer and consumer accounts
explain: AWS PrivateLink requires a two-sided configuration: the producer creates an endpoint service backed by a Network Load Balancer and grants permission for consumer accounts to connect; the consumer then creates an Interface VPC Endpoint in their VPC that connects to the producer's service. VPC peering (option D) is transitive routing between VPCs and does not provide the service abstraction, centralized access control, or independent CIDR that PrivateLink offers.

q: A company implementing PrivateLink for internal services identifies a critical missing component. What is essential for the architecture to function?
options: Producer-side Network Load Balancer fronting the service
Route 53 health checks on the producer service
Consumer security group configuration only
Producer service endpoint AND consumer interface endpoint configured in the consumer VPC
explain: Both sides of the PrivateLink configuration must be present: the producer must create an endpoint service (backed by an NLB) and the consumer must create an Interface VPC Endpoint that references the producer's service name. The NLB on the producer side (option A) is a necessary component but insufficient alone — without the consumer interface endpoint, no ENI exists in the consumer VPC to receive traffic.

q: A company configuring Transit Gateway finds that spoke VPCs cannot reach shared services in a central VPC. What configuration is required?
options: Shared services VPC with Transit Gateway attachment and route table entries in all spoke VPCs pointing to the Transit Gateway
Direct VPN connection from each spoke to the shared services VPC
Transit Gateway does not support shared services routing
Replace Transit Gateway with VPC peering
explain: For spoke VPCs to reach a shared services VPC via Transit Gateway, two route entries are required: (1) the Transit Gateway route table must have a route for the shared services CIDR pointing to its attachment, and (2) each spoke VPC's route table must have a route for the shared services CIDR pointing to the Transit Gateway. Missing either direction causes a routing failure even though the attachments are in AVAILABLE state.

q: A startup designing its network for rapid growth requires CIDR flexibility. What VPC CIDR strategy enables this?
options: Add secondary blocks reactively when the primary is exhausted
Request a new VPC each time capacity is needed
Use a minimal /28 CIDR and expand later
Plan primary and secondary CIDR ranges hierarchically from the beginning
explain: Planning secondary CIDR ranges before they are needed ensures they are compatible with existing VPC peering connections and Transit Gateway routing, which impose the constraint that attached VPCs cannot have overlapping CIDRs. Reactive secondary CIDR selection (option A) often discovers that preferred ranges conflict with existing routes, requiring manual CIDR coordination across multiple accounts. A /28 (option C) provides only 11 usable IPs — completely insufficient for any meaningful workload.

q: A company designing a VPC endpoint service realizes that consumer accounts need to connect to the producer's PrivateLink service. What action do consumers take?
options: Request access permission from the producer account
Create an Interface VPC Endpoint in their VPC referencing the producer's service name
Configure NAT to the producer service's IP address
Establish VPC peering with the producer VPC
explain: Consumers connect to a PrivateLink service by creating an Interface VPC Endpoint in their VPC and specifying the producer's endpoint service name (e.g., com.amazonaws.vpce.us-east-1.vpce-svc-xxxxx). This creates an ENI in the consumer's subnet with a private IP, and all traffic to that IP is forwarded through the AWS PrivateLink fabric to the producer's NLB. VPC peering (option D) requires non-overlapping CIDRs and creates a bidirectional route, unlike the unidirectional PrivateLink model.

q: A company implementing global networking needs different routing behaviors per region. What enables this?
options: Identical Transit Gateway configurations in every region
Regional Transit Gateways with inter-region peering and region-specific route tables
A single Transit Gateway deployed globally
Separate networks with no inter-region connectivity
explain: Each AWS region has its own Transit Gateway with independent route tables. Inter-region peering connects regional TGWs while preserving each region's independent routing policies. For example, us-east-1 might route all egress through a security VPC while eu-west-1 routes through a separate compliance inspection path. A single global Transit Gateway does not exist in AWS — TGWs are regional resources.

q: A company implementing multi-region connectivity via Transit Gateway peering finds no traffic flows despite the peering attachment being accepted. What is missing?
options: The peering has already been accepted, which should be sufficient
Transit Gateway attachment is in AVAILABLE state
Route table entries in both regions' Transit Gateway route tables pointing to the peering attachment are missing
An Internet Gateway must be attached to one of the Transit Gateways
explain: Accepting a Transit Gateway peering request puts the attachment in AVAILABLE state but does not create any routes. Administrators must manually add static routes in each region's TGW route table pointing the remote region's CIDR to the peering attachment. This is different from VPC attachments where route propagation can be enabled — TGW-to-TGW peering always requires manual static routes in both route tables.

q: A company initializes a VPC for a multi-tier application across 8 AZs and 2 regions. What is the minimum subnet count needed?
options: One subnet per AZ
Three subnets per AZ (public, private, protected)
Two subnets per AZ (public, private)
Three subnets per AZ per region minimum
explain: Proper multi-tier design requires one subnet per tier per AZ for independent NACL enforcement and fault isolation. With 3 tiers (web, application, database), 8 AZs, and 2 regions, the minimum is 3 × 8 × 2 = 48 subnets. Option B (three subnets per AZ without specifying per region) gives 24 subnets — missing the full regional coverage. AWS best practice is to place subnets in every AZ within a region for maximum fault tolerance.

q: A company needs to connect 100 branch offices to AWS. What architectural challenge makes full mesh peering impractical?
options: Site-to-Site VPN from each branch to AWS Transit Gateway
VPC peering between all branch VPCs
Transit Gateway with 100 VPN attachments
100 branches in full mesh requires 4,950 peering connections — unmanageable at scale
explain: Full mesh between n nodes requires n*(n-1)/2 connections: 100 branches = 4,950 peering connections, each requiring bilateral route table entries. Transit Gateway replaces this with 100 VPN attachments and a single centralized route table, reducing management complexity from 9,900 route entries (4,950 × 2 sides) to approximately 200. Options A and C are both correct Transit Gateway approaches — this question asks what makes full mesh impractical.

q: A company implementing compliance-driven three-tier network segmentation (internet-facing, application, database) realizes a fourth tier is missing. What additional tier is required?
options: Encrypting all traffic between tiers
Adding WAF to the internet-facing tier
Using separate VPCs for each tier
A dedicated management tier with separate, restricted connectivity for administrative access
explain: Modern compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, NIST) require a separate management/bastion tier with restricted access controls for administrative operations. Without this tier, admin access (SSH, RDP, AWS SSM) flows through the same network paths as application traffic, complicating audit trails and violating least-privilege network access principles. The management tier typically has a separate security group and NACL that only permits access from known admin IP ranges.

q: A company with global offices implementing Direct Connect realizes a critical redundancy element is missing. What is required for true redundancy?
options: LAG within a single Direct Connect location
VPN backup only
Multiple Direct Connect connections at diverse AWS locations using different carriers
One Direct Connect connection per AWS region
explain: Geographic diversity is the critical missing element: a LAG at a single location (option A) protects against port failure but not facility outage or carrier disruption. True redundancy requires diverse physical paths — different AWS Direct Connect locations in different buildings with different carriers. AWS's Direct Connect resiliency recommendations (Maximum Resiliency model) specifically call for connections at multiple locations with no shared physical components.

q: A company extending its VPC architecture to 10,000 instances discovers subnet IP exhaustion. What is the root cause?
options: Lack of hierarchical growth planning with secondary CIDR blocks reserved from the start
Instances cannot be added to an existing VPC
Using too many small subnets
The initial CIDR block was too small
explain: IP exhaustion in a growing VPC typically results from not reserving secondary CIDR blocks during initial planning. While the root cause may be a small initial CIDR (option D), the problem is the absence of a growth plan — specifically, failing to pre-allocate secondary CIDR ranges from non-conflicting RFC 1918 space before they are needed. Once peering connections and Transit Gateway routes exist, adding a compatible secondary CIDR becomes a coordination challenge across multiple teams.

q: A company configures a Customer Gateway for a Site-to-Site VPN but BGP fails to establish. Logs show an ASN mismatch. What is the issue?
options: VPN tunnel is DOWN
Route table does not have BGP propagation enabled
Border router does not support BGP
Virtual Private Gateway and Customer Gateway have different BGP Autonomous System Numbers configured
explain: BGP requires both peers to agree on each other's ASN before a session can be established. The Virtual Private Gateway has a default ASN of 64512 (or a custom ASN if specified at creation), and the Customer Gateway must be configured with the matching remote ASN. An ASN mismatch causes BGP OPEN messages to be rejected, preventing the session from transitioning to the ESTABLISHED state. VPN tunnel DOWN (option A) would prevent BGP from even attempting, but the question states BGP fails — implying the tunnel is up.

q: A network engineer configures a Direct Connect Virtual Interface and the VIF shows UP, but routes are not appearing in the VPC route table. What is needed?
options: Routes must be explicitly propagated from the Virtual Private Gateway or added manually
Routes are automatically added by AWS when the VIF comes up
Routes appear automatically after the BGP session establishes
Wait 15 minutes for automatic route synchronization
explain: Even with a BGP session established on the Direct Connect VIF and routes being advertised, those routes do not appear in the VPC route table until route propagation is explicitly enabled for the Virtual Private Gateway on that route table. Navigate to VPC > Route Tables, select the route table, and enable Propagate Routes from the VGW. Without propagation enabled, routes must be added as static entries. Automatic propagation (options B, C, D) does not occur.

q: A company implements AWS Client VPN with split tunneling to reduce bandwidth usage on the VPN. What controls which traffic routes through the VPN tunnel?
options: Endpoint route table filtering rules
Security group rules on the VPN endpoint
Client VPN endpoint route configuration specifying which destination CIDRs traverse the tunnel
Authentication authorization rules
explain: Split tunneling in AWS Client VPN is controlled by the routes added to the Client VPN endpoint. Only traffic destined for CIDRs explicitly listed as routes on the endpoint traverses the VPN tunnel — all other traffic exits through the client's local internet connection. In full-tunnel mode, a default route (0.0.0.0/0) is added, forcing all traffic through VPN. Security group rules (option B) control what VPN clients can access within the VPC, not which traffic enters the tunnel.

q: A network architect implements VPC peering between two VPCs. Traffic fails despite security groups permitting the traffic. What is missing?
options: Internet Gateway attachment to both VPCs
Peering connection acceptance by the peer VPC owner
Network ACL rules on both VPCs
Route table entries on BOTH VPCs pointing to the peering connection
explain: VPC peering creates a logical network link but does not automatically create any routes. Both VPCs must have explicit route table entries: VPC-A's route table must have a route for VPC-B's CIDR pointing to the peering connection ID, and VPC-B's route table must have the reciprocal route. Missing routes in either direction cause one-way or complete traffic failures even when security groups and NACLs permit the traffic.

q: A company configures multiple Direct Connect Virtual Interfaces on one physical connection. How are they logically separated?
options: IEEE 802.1Q VLAN tagging on the same physical port
BGP AS path prepending on separate connections
Separate IP address ranges for each VIF
Separate physical ports for each VIF
explain: A single AWS Direct Connect physical connection supports multiple Virtual Interfaces using IEEE 802.1Q VLAN tagging. Each VIF is assigned a unique VLAN ID (between 1 and 4094), and the customer's router must tag traffic with the correct VLAN ID for each VIF. This allows one 10 Gbps port to simultaneously carry a private VIF (to a VPC), a public VIF (to AWS public services like S3), and a transit VIF (to Transit Gateway). BGP AS path prepending (option B) is a route preference mechanism, not a VIF separation mechanism.

q: A multinational financial services company operates across 15 AWS regions with 50 VPCs and plans to scale to 200 VPCs. Which CIDR planning approach ensures no overlapping address spaces?
options: Use /24 blocks to maximize subnet density per VPC
Use a /8 block globally and subdivide hierarchically into /18 per region with secondary blocks reserved
Use /16 independently per region with no cross-region planning
Use overlapping blocks and rely on NAT to prevent conflicts
explain: A /8 global allocation (16.7M addresses) divided into /18 per region (262k per region) provides ample space for 200 VPCs across 15 regions while keeping all addresses within a single summarizable prefix. Hierarchical planning ensures that Transit Gateway inter-region peering works without CIDR conflicts. Independent /16 per region (option C) risks conflicts when teams in different regions independently choose the same ranges, which is common without a central IPAM.

q: A network engineer enables route propagation from a Direct Connect VGW but static routes in the route table override the propagated routes. What causes this behavior?
options: Static routes always have higher priority than propagated BGP routes in AWS route tables
VPN routes are preferred over Direct Connect routes
BGP advertisements are incorrectly configured
Route propagation is disabled on the virtual interface
explain: AWS route table evaluation follows a strict priority order: static routes (most specific match) always override propagated routes from a Virtual Private Gateway. This is intentional — static routes represent explicit administrative intent. To allow BGP-propagated routes to take effect for a destination, remove any conflicting static routes. If both a static /16 and a propagated /24 exist for the same destination, the more specific /24 wins regardless of type.

q: A company implements AWS Client VPN but users cannot access any resources in the VPC. What is the most likely missing configuration?
options: Route 53 DNS misconfiguration
Mutual TLS certificate not installed on clients
SAML authentication provider not configured
Target network association and authorization rules are missing
explain: AWS Client VPN requires two configurations beyond the endpoint itself: (1) a target network association linking the endpoint to a subnet in the VPC so that client traffic can enter the VPC, and (2) authorization rules that explicitly grant the client IP CIDR access to specific destination CIDRs. Without both, VPN clients connect successfully (tunnel is up) but receive no network access. DNS issues (option A) would affect name resolution but not all connectivity.

q: A network architect configures Direct Connect Link Aggregation Group (LAG) for bandwidth aggregation. What is the minimum number of connections required?
options: Four connections
A single connection with increased port speed
Any number up to the LAG port limit
Two connections
explain: AWS Direct Connect LAG requires a minimum of 2 connections to aggregate bandwidth and provide link-level redundancy. All LAG member connections must have identical speed and terminate at the same AWS Direct Connect location. A single connection with higher speed (option B) does not constitute a LAG and provides no link redundancy. AWS supports LAG groups of up to 4 connections, each of the same port speed (1 Gbps or 10 Gbps).

q: A company configures IPSec VPN tunnels but observes packet loss. The MTU on both endpoints is set to 1500 bytes. What causes this?
options: IPSec encryption overhead exceeds the 1500-byte MTU, causing fragmentation and potential packet loss
BGP session flapping on the VPN tunnel
BGP route conflicts between tunnel endpoints
MTU size mismatch between tunnel endpoints
explain: IPSec in tunnel mode adds approximately 50–80 bytes of overhead (IP header, ESP header, IV, ICV) to each packet. With a 1500-byte payload MTU and 1500-byte link MTU, the encrypted packet exceeds 1500 bytes and must be fragmented. Many networks and firewalls drop fragmented IPSec packets, causing packet loss. The solution is to set the tunnel interface MTU to 1436–1450 bytes so that encrypted packets fit within the 1500-byte outer MTU.

q: A network engineer implements Transit Gateway with VPC attachments but traffic between attached VPCs fails. What is misconfigured?
options: Attachments are not propagating routes to the TGW route table
Transit Gateway route table lacks routes for the destination VPC CIDRs
Transit Gateway is in a failed state
Attachment associations are invalid
explain: AWS Transit Gateway does not automatically create routes between attachments. The TGW route table must contain routes for each destination VPC's CIDR pointing to that VPC's attachment. Without these routes (either static or propagated), traffic arriving at the TGW has no forwarding entry and is dropped. Separately, each spoke VPC's route table must also have a route pointing its destinations to the TGW. Both sides must be configured.

q: A company establishes a Direct Connect connection but experiences periodic latency spikes during peak hours. What configuration addresses this?
options: Configuring a Site-to-Site VPN as a backup for Direct Connect failover
Increasing the Direct Connect port speed
Enabling jumbo frames on the Direct Connect connection
Provisioning additional Direct Connect VIFs at diverse locations for load distribution
explain: Latency spikes on a Direct Connect connection during peak hours indicate congestion, which cannot be resolved by a single VIF regardless of jumbo frame settings. Provisioning additional VIFs at diverse Direct Connect locations distributes traffic across multiple physical paths, and BGP multipath (ECMP) or traffic engineering can balance load. Jumbo frames (option C) reduce per-packet overhead but do not address bandwidth congestion causing latency spikes.

q: A network engineer configures BGP route propagation on a Virtual Private Gateway, but routes do not appear in the route table even though the BGP session is established. What is the issue?
options: BGP ASN conflicts between VGW and Customer Gateway
Security group rules are blocking BGP port 179
The VPN tunnel is DOWN
Route propagation is not enabled on the route table for this Virtual Private Gateway
explain: Route propagation from a Virtual Private Gateway must be explicitly enabled on each route table that should receive BGP-advertised routes. Even with an established BGP session actively advertising routes, those routes do not appear in any VPC route table until propagation is enabled for the VGW on that specific route table. This is configured in the VPC console under Route Tables > Route Propagation. Security groups (option B) do not affect BGP sessions in AWS — BGP runs within the VGW, not through security groups.

q: A company deploys AWS Client VPN but users cannot access resources in a private subnet despite a connected tunnel. What is the issue?
options: Route 53 DNS resolution issues
Mutual TLS authentication failure
Target network association is missing or authorization rules are blocking access to the subnet CIDR
SAML identity provider timeout
explain: AWS Client VPN requires two explicit configurations for VPC resource access: (1) a target network association connecting the endpoint to a specific subnet so traffic can enter the VPC, and (2) authorization rules granting the client address range access to the destination subnet CIDR. When users connect successfully but cannot reach resources, the authorization rule is typically missing. DNS issues (option A) would cause name resolution failures, not complete connectivity failures.

q: A company configures BGP on Direct Connect with both Direct Connect and a VPN backup. Traffic should prefer Direct Connect during normal operation and fail over to VPN automatically. How is this configured?
options: Equal-cost routing in the VPC route table
BGP local preference with a higher value on Direct Connect routes
AS path prepending on the Direct Connect advertisements
Disable BGP on the Direct Connect during VPN testing
explain: BGP local preference is an intra-AS attribute used to influence outbound path selection — a higher local preference value causes BGP to prefer that path. Setting local preference to 200 on routes learned via Direct Connect and 100 on VPN routes makes all outbound traffic prefer Direct Connect. When the Direct Connect VIF fails, BGP withdraws those routes and traffic automatically shifts to the lower-preference VPN routes without any manual intervention.

q: A company configures a NAT Gateway in a private subnet but instances in that private subnet cannot reach the internet. What is the configuration flaw?
options: NAT Gateway must be deployed in a public subnet with an Elastic IP address
NAT Gateway cannot be placed in private subnets under any circumstances
An Internet Gateway must be attached separately to the NAT Gateway
NAT Gateway requires a security group to allow outbound traffic
explain: A NAT Gateway must be deployed in a public subnet — a subnet with a route to an Internet Gateway — because the NAT Gateway itself needs internet access to perform network address translation. It is assigned an Elastic IP address for outbound connectivity. Private subnet instances route their outbound traffic to the NAT Gateway, which then uses its Elastic IP to communicate with the internet via the Internet Gateway. NAT Gateways do not use security groups (option D) — they are managed by AWS.

q: A network engineer needs to enable IPv6 outbound-only internet access for instances in a private subnet. What component is required?
options: NAT Gateway with IPv6 support
Egress-Only Internet Gateway with a route table entry for ::/0
Route 53 Resolver for IPv6
VPN endpoint with IPv6 configuration
explain: An Egress-Only Internet Gateway (EIGW) is the IPv6 equivalent of a NAT Gateway: it allows IPv6 instances in private subnets to initiate outbound connections to the internet while blocking all unsolicited inbound traffic. Unlike NAT, which performs address translation, EIGW passes IPv6 addresses unchanged but acts as a stateful gateway allowing only return traffic for established connections. The route table must have a ::/0 route pointing to the EIGW.

q: A company requires VPN redundancy with automatic failover and no manual intervention. How should connections be configured?
options: Single VPN connection with load balancing across both tunnels
Multiple VPN connections to separate Customer Gateways with dynamic BGP routing
Multiple VPN connections with identical BGP weights
Single VPN connection relying on the two built-in redundant tunnels
explain: True VPN redundancy requires separate Customer Gateway devices — ideally at different physical locations — each with its own VPN connection to AWS. BGP dynamic routing enables automatic failover: when one Customer Gateway becomes unreachable, BGP withdraws its routes and traffic automatically flows through the surviving connection. A single VPN connection with two tunnels (option D) protects against AWS-side tunnel failures but not Customer Gateway device failures.

q: A company configures multiple Direct Connect Virtual Interfaces on one physical connection. How are they logically separated on the wire?
options: IEEE 802.1Q VLAN tagging assigns each VIF a unique VLAN ID
Assigning different IP address ranges to each VIF for isolation
Separate physical ports for each VIF at the colocation facility
BGP AS path prepending to distinguish traffic per VIF
explain: IEEE 802.1Q VLAN tagging is the mechanism that separates multiple VIFs on a single Direct Connect physical connection. Each VIF receives a unique VLAN tag between 1 and 4094, and the customer's router must tag egress frames with the correct VLAN ID. This allows one 10 Gbps physical port to simultaneously carry a private VIF to a VPC (tagged with VLAN 100), a public VIF to AWS services (VLAN 200), and a transit VIF to Transit Gateway (VLAN 300) without interference.

q: A network engineer establishes a Direct Connect public VIF but cannot reach S3 public IPs from on-premises. The VIF is UP and the BGP session is established. What is missing?
options: S3 requires an Interface VPC Endpoint, not a public VIF
S3 IP prefixes are not advertised over the public VIF
AWS advertises S3 IP prefixes via the public VIF but the on-premises route table lacks an entry directing S3 traffic to the Direct Connect interface
AWS does not advertise S3 service prefixes via Direct Connect
explain: An AWS Direct Connect public VIF does advertise AWS public service IP prefixes (including S3) via BGP. However, on-premises routers must accept and install those BGP-learned prefixes into their routing table and have the correct interface pointing to the Direct Connect BGP peer. If the on-premises routing table still has a default route pointing to the internet, S3 traffic follows the default route instead of Direct Connect. The fix is ensuring BGP-learned S3 prefixes are preferred over the internet default route.

q: A company implements AWS Client VPN with mutual TLS authentication. What certificates must be present on the client device?
options: Client certificate, private key, and CA certificate
Only the client certificate
Only the CA certificate
Client certificate and private key only (no CA certificate)
explain: Mutual TLS (mTLS) requires the client to authenticate to the server and the server to authenticate to the client. The client needs: (1) its own client certificate (issued by the CA registered with Client VPN) to present for authentication, (2) the private key corresponding to that certificate to prove ownership, and (3) the CA certificate to validate the VPN server's certificate during the TLS handshake. Without the CA certificate, the client cannot verify the server's identity and will reject the connection.

q: A network architect implements VPC peering across 10 VPCs. What is the primary operational limitation?
options: CIDR blocks must be identical across all peered VPCs
All VPCs must be in the same AWS region
Full mesh peering requires 45 connections and route table entries in every VPC; Transit Gateway reduces this to 10 attachments
VPC peering cannot handle more than 10 VPCs
explain: The operational limitation of VPC peering at scale is combinatorial complexity: 10 VPCs require n*(n-1)/2 = 45 peering connections, each requiring bilateral route table entries in both VPCs. This results in 90 route table updates for a complete mesh. More critically, VPC peering is non-transitive — VPC A peered with B and B peered with C does not give A connectivity to C. Transit Gateway solves both problems with 10 attachments and a single route table.

q: A company configures a backup Site-to-Site VPN alongside Direct Connect. How should routing preference be configured so Direct Connect is always primary?
options: Equal-cost load balancing between both connections
VPN as primary, Direct Connect as backup
BGP local preference with a higher value on Direct Connect routes than VPN routes
Equal BGP weights in the route table
explain: BGP local preference determines path selection when multiple valid routes exist for the same destination. Setting local preference to 200 on routes received via Direct Connect and 100 on VPN routes makes the Virtual Private Gateway prefer Direct Connect for all outbound traffic. On Direct Connect failure, BGP withdraws the high-preference routes and VPN routes with lower preference automatically become active — no manual intervention required.

q: A company configures AWS Client VPN with SAML 2.0 federated authentication. What advantage does this provide over mutual TLS?
options: RADIUS authentication for legacy systems
Active Directory authentication only
Mutual TLS with simpler certificate management
SAML 2.0 federation integrates with cloud IdPs (Okta, Azure AD), supports MFA, and eliminates per-user certificate distribution
explain: SAML 2.0 federated authentication for Client VPN allows users to authenticate using their existing corporate identity provider credentials, which already enforces MFA policies. Unlike mutual TLS, there is no per-user certificate to generate, distribute, revoke, or renew. When an employee leaves, disabling their IdP account immediately revokes VPN access. RADIUS (option A) is a separate authentication protocol that also supports Active Directory but lacks the SSO and MFA federation capabilities of SAML 2.0.

q: A network engineer implements Transit Gateway with VPC attachments. Traffic between spoke VPCs fails despite attachments being in AVAILABLE state. What is missing?
options: An Internet Gateway must be attached to Transit Gateway
Attachments must be manually put into AVAILABLE state
BGP sessions must be established between Transit Gateway and each VPC
Transit Gateway route table must have routes for each spoke VPC CIDR, or route propagation must be enabled
explain: Transit Gateway does not automatically create forwarding routes when VPC attachments are created. Either static routes must be added to the TGW route table for each spoke VPC's CIDR pointing to its attachment, or route propagation must be enabled so the TGW automatically learns each VPC's CIDR from the attachment metadata. Additionally, each spoke VPC's route table must have a route pointing destination CIDRs toward the Transit Gateway. Both directions must be configured.

q: A company establishes a shared services VPC accessible to all spoke VPCs via Transit Gateway. What configuration is required?
options: VPC peering between each spoke and shared services VPC
VPC peering between each spoke and shared services only
Site-to-Site VPN connections from each spoke
Shared services VPC attachment to TGW with routes in both the TGW route table and each spoke VPC's route table
explain: Shared services via Transit Gateway requires bidirectional route configuration: the TGW route table must have a route for the shared services CIDR pointing to its attachment, and each spoke VPC's route table must have a route for the shared services CIDR pointing to the TGW. Additionally, the shared services VPC's route table needs routes back to each spoke VPC CIDR via TGW (or a more specific summary route). Missing any of these three route sets breaks connectivity.

q: A company configures a Virtual Private Gateway with route propagation enabled but static routes override the BGP-propagated routes. Why?
options: BGP-propagated routes are always preferred over static routes
A BGP session failure caused routes to be withdrawn
Static routes always have higher routing priority than VGW-propagated routes in AWS route tables
The VGW is misconfigured
explain: AWS route table evaluation gives static routes unconditional priority over propagated routes for the same destination. This is by design: static routes represent explicit administrative intent that should override dynamically learned routes. To allow BGP-propagated routes to control routing for a destination, delete any conflicting static routes from the route table. This is distinct from BGP decision-making on the VGW itself — the route table priority is an AWS routing construct.

q: A network team analyzes VPC Flow Logs and identifies what appears to be unauthorized traffic. What must be verified before implementing blocking rules?
options: Confirm the flow log format is correctly parsed
Confirm Flow Logs are enabled on all ENIs
Confirm CloudWatch log retention is sufficient
Confirm the source and destination IPs are genuinely unauthorized and not legitimate traffic
explain: Before blocking traffic based on VPC Flow Log data, the team must verify that the flagged source and destination IPs are genuinely unauthorized. Flow logs record all traffic including legitimate administrative access, monitoring systems, and health checks. Blocking based on unverified IP analysis risks disrupting legitimate services. Cross-reference the IPs against known asset inventories, DNS records, and application dependency maps before implementing security group or NACL blocks.

q: A network team enables VPC Flow Logs at the subnet level. What is a key limitation of subnet-level versus ENI-level logging?
options: Subnet-level logs miss traffic to individual instances within the subnet
Subnet-level logs cannot differentiate which specific instance or ENI generated a flow within the subnet
Subnet-level logs capture less detailed format information
Subnet-level logs are more expensive than ENI-level logs
explain: VPC Flow Logs at the subnet level capture all traffic crossing the subnet boundary but aggregate it at the subnet level, making it impossible to identify which specific EC2 instance or ENI generated a particular flow. For forensic investigation of a compromised instance, ENI-level flow logs are required because they include the network interface ID, allowing correlation with specific instances. Subnet-level logs are sufficient for traffic volume analysis but inadequate for per-instance security investigations.

q: A company uses VPC Reachability Analyzer and gets a REACHABLE result, but the application connection still fails. Why might this occur?
options: Reachability Analyzer has inaccurate results for this network configuration
Layer 3/4 network connectivity exists but application-layer issues (TLS, authentication, application errors) prevent the connection
The instance's security group was modified after the test completed
The RDS security group changed after the reachability test ran
explain: AWS Reachability Analyzer performs static analysis of network configuration (route tables, security groups, NACLs, internet gateways) to determine if a network path exists at Layer 3/4. A REACHABLE result confirms packets can flow from source to destination but does not validate Layer 7 behavior: TLS certificate validation failures, application authentication errors, incorrect application port configuration, and database credential issues all cause connection failures despite a REACHABLE status.

q: A company must monitor egress bandwidth with per-instance visibility for cost control. What metric provides this?
options: NetworkPacketsOut per instance
BytesSentToDestination from the ALB access logs
EC2 NetworkOut metric per instance in CloudWatch
NetworkIn and NetworkOut aggregate totals
explain: The EC2 NetworkOut CloudWatch metric provides per-instance outbound byte counts at 1-minute or 5-minute resolution, enabling identification of which specific instances generate the most outbound traffic. This granularity allows teams to optimize data transfer costs by targeting specific workloads. VPC-level or ALB-level aggregate metrics (options B, D) cannot pinpoint individual instance behavior. NetworkPacketsOut (option A) counts packets, not bytes — insufficient for cost analysis.

q: A network engineer enables Traffic Mirroring for monitoring encrypted traffic. Where is the traffic filter applied?
options: On the mirror session's target ENI
On the mirror target network interface at the appliance
On the source ENI's egress filter configuration in the mirror session
On both source and destination ENIs simultaneously
explain: AWS Traffic Mirroring filters are defined in a mirror filter resource and applied to the mirror session on the source ENI. The filter specifies which traffic to copy — by protocol, source/destination port ranges, and direction (ingress/egress). Only matching traffic is VXLAN-encapsulated and sent to the mirror target, reducing unnecessary load on the analysis appliance. Applying filters at the source ENI (not the target) ensures the analysis tool receives only relevant traffic.

q: A company requires DNS query monitoring for security investigation. What is the best architecture?
options: Route 53 Resolver query logging to CloudWatch Logs, S3, or Kinesis Firehose
Traffic Mirroring to a DNS analyzer appliance
VPC Flow Logs filtered for port 53 traffic
CloudWatch Application Insights for DNS patterns
explain: Route 53 Resolver query logging captures every DNS query made by resources within a VPC, including the query name, type, response, source IP, and timestamp. This provides security visibility into DNS-based C2 communication, data exfiltration via DNS tunneling, and unauthorized lookups. VPC Flow Logs (option C) only show that a UDP/53 connection occurred — they do not capture the actual domain name queried, making them insufficient for DNS security monitoring.

q: A network team observes asymmetric routing where forward and return traffic take different paths. What diagnostic approach identifies the root cause?
options: Enable VPC Flow Logs on all subnets and look for REJECT entries
Increase Direct Connect bandwidth on one path
Analyze BGP routing tables and advertisements on both AWS and on-premises sides simultaneously
Increase DNS TTL values in Route 53
explain: Asymmetric routing occurs when different BGP attributes (MED, local preference, AS path length) cause traffic to take different forward and return paths. Diagnosing it requires comparing the BGP routing table on the AWS side (VGW or TGW route tables) with the on-premises router's BGP table to identify which routes differ. For example, the on-premises router might prefer the Direct Connect path outbound, while AWS prefers the VPN path for return traffic due to mismatched MED values.

q: A company implements AWS Network Access Analyzer. What does it NOT evaluate?
options: Encryption protocol standards compliance
BGP route propagation timing
Layer 7 application traffic patterns and content
Network path configuration (routes, security groups, NACLs)
explain: AWS Network Access Analyzer performs static analysis of network configuration resources (security groups, NACLs, route tables, internet gateways, VPC endpoints) to determine if network paths comply with access policies. It does not inspect live traffic, cannot analyze application-layer content, and has no visibility into HTTP payloads, TLS certificates, or application behavior. Network Access Analyzer is a configuration analysis tool, not a traffic inspection tool.

q: A network engineer troubleshoots high inter-AZ latency. What is the most direct measurement approach?
options: RTT measurements using ping or TCP handshake timing between instances in each AZ
Direct Connect connection latency metrics
VPC Flow Logs analysis for TCP retransmissions
NetworkLatency CloudWatch metric for inter-AZ traffic
explain: AWS does not publish a dedicated CloudWatch metric for inter-AZ latency at the VPC level. The most direct measurement is deploying test instances in each AZ and measuring round-trip time using ICMP ping or TCP connection timing. Tools like iperf3 (for throughput and latency) or hping3 (for TCP-level RTT) provide accurate measurements. AWS inter-AZ latency is typically 1–2ms within a region; values significantly higher indicate a configuration or hardware issue.

q: A company monitors NAT Gateway metrics and observes a high connection count. Which metric specifically indicates port exhaustion?
options: ErrorPortAllocation metric in CloudWatch
PacketsDropCount metric
ConnectionAttemptCount metric
BytesOutToDestination metric
explain: The ErrorPortAllocation CloudWatch metric specifically tracks the number of times a NAT Gateway failed to allocate a port for a new connection. Each NAT Gateway supports up to 55,000 simultaneous connections per unique destination IP/port combination per Elastic IP. When this limit is reached, new connection attempts fail and ErrorPortAllocation increments. Remediation options include adding additional NAT Gateways per subnet or adding multiple Elastic IPs to an existing NAT Gateway.

q: A company deploys Traffic Mirroring to a network analysis appliance. What throughput limitation must be accounted for?
options: Total mirrored throughput is limited to 10 Gbps aggregate
Throughput is limited by the source ENI bandwidth
Throughput is limited by the mirror target's capacity (instance type or NLB limits)
Traffic Mirroring has unlimited throughput
explain: AWS Traffic Mirroring is bounded by the capacity of the mirror target: if the target is an EC2 instance running a network appliance, its instance type's network bandwidth limit applies. If the target is a Network Load Balancer, NLB throughput limits apply. When mirroring high-bandwidth source ENIs, the target must be sized to handle the full mirrored volume — otherwise, the appliance becomes a bottleneck and drops mirrored packets, causing gaps in security analysis.

q: A network team analyzes VPC Flow Logs to establish baseline traffic patterns. What combination best identifies anomalies?
options: Bytes count trending alone
Flow record count alone
Both bytes transferred and packet counts compared against historical baselines
Packet count trending alone
explain: Effective anomaly detection requires combining multiple metrics because different attack patterns manifest differently: a DDoS scan shows high packet count with low bytes (many small probes), while data exfiltration shows high bytes with normal packet count (large file transfers). Comparing both metrics against historical baselines reveals anomalies that single-metric analysis misses. CloudWatch Metrics Insights or Athena queries against S3-stored flow logs enable this combined baseline comparison.

q: A company monitors Direct Connect but notices that connection-level metrics are missing from CloudWatch. Why?
options: The CloudWatch agent must be installed on the Direct Connect router
Direct Connect metrics are not available in CloudWatch
Direct Connect CloudWatch metrics must be explicitly enabled for the connection
Direct Connect metrics are only available at the LOA level
explain: AWS Direct Connect connection-level CloudWatch metrics (ConnectionBpsEgress, ConnectionBpsIngress, ConnectionCRCErrorCount, ConnectionLightLevelRx, ConnectionState) require explicit enablement in the Direct Connect console or API. Once enabled, metrics begin reporting. This is different from most other AWS services where CloudWatch metrics are enabled by default. The requirement to explicitly enable monitoring is a common source of missing Direct Connect metrics.

q: A network engineer monitors Traffic Mirroring logs and observes duplicate packets at the analysis appliance. What causes this?
options: Multiple mirror sessions on the same source ENI with overlapping or identical filters
The source ENI is duplicating packets before mirroring
The mirror filter is capturing both ingress and egress of the same flow
The mirror target is echoing packets back to the source
explain: If two or more Traffic Mirror sessions are configured on the same source ENI with identical or overlapping filters, each session independently copies and forwards the matching packets to its target. The analysis appliance receives the same packet multiple times — once per session that captured it. The solution is to audit all mirror sessions on the ENI, ensure filters are non-overlapping, and consolidate sessions where possible.

q: A company implements Network Access Analyzer and receives warnings about internet reachability. What is the appropriate first response?
options: Enable Shield Advanced immediately for all internet-reachable resources
Verify whether the internet access is intentional before taking any remediation action
Immediately block all inbound internet access
Disable the internet-reachable instances
explain: Network Access Analyzer findings indicate that a network path exists — not that it is a security violation. Internet-reachable instances may be intentionally public-facing (web servers, bastion hosts, ALBs). The correct process is to cross-reference findings with the application architecture, confirm whether each reachable resource is supposed to be internet-accessible, and only then remediate unintended exposure. Immediately blocking (option C) risks taking down legitimate public services.

q: A network team monitors Direct Connect with decreasing throughput. What diagnostic approach identifies the congestion location?
options: AWS CloudWatch metrics for the Direct Connect connection only
On-premises interface statistics only
Comparing both AWS CloudWatch Direct Connect metrics and on-premises router interface statistics
BGP timer analysis on the virtual interface
explain: Locating a Direct Connect throughput bottleneck requires comparing metrics from both endpoints. AWS CloudWatch shows connection-level ingress/egress throughput from the AWS perspective, while on-premises interface counters show utilization from the customer side. If AWS shows normal utilization while on-premises shows saturation, the bottleneck is on the customer's equipment. If both show normal but application throughput is low, the bottleneck is in the application itself or BGP routing.

q: A company implements AWS Network Firewall but observes excessive legitimate traffic being blocked. What is the immediate corrective action?
options: Disable the Network Firewall
Analyze blocked traffic logs and refine rules to reduce false positives
Increase rule group priorities
Replace Network Firewall with security groups
explain: When Network Firewall produces excessive false positives, the correct response is to analyze the firewall logs (in CloudWatch Logs or S3) to identify exactly which rules are triggering on legitimate traffic. Rules can then be refined by adding allow rules for legitimate patterns or temporarily changing blocking rules to Alert-only mode to observe impact without dropping traffic. Disabling the firewall (option A) removes all protection and is never the appropriate response to a tuning issue.

q: A financial services company configures Network Firewall for intrusion detection. What rule type provides the most effective IDS/IPS capability?
options: Application layer rules only
Stateless packet header rules
Stateful rules with Suricata-compatible IDS/IPS signatures
Domain list blocking rules
explain: AWS Network Firewall stateful rules support the Suricata-compatible rule format, which enables importing industry-standard threat intelligence signatures from sources like ET Open and Proofpoint Emerging Threats. Suricata rules provide deep packet inspection, protocol anomaly detection, and pattern matching against known attack payloads. Stateless rules (option B) only inspect packet headers (IP, TCP flags) and cannot detect application-layer attacks or examine payload content where intrusion signatures appear.

q: A company uses WAF to protect APIs. SQL injection still bypasses WAF rules. What additional protection layer is required?
options: Additional WAF managed rule groups for SQL injection
Network Firewall with stateless rules
Shield Advanced
Application-layer input validation with parameterized queries
explain: WAF SQL injection rules block common attack patterns in HTTP request parameters and headers, but they cannot protect against context-specific SQL injection in custom application logic, stored procedures, or second-order injection. Application-layer parameterized queries (prepared statements) are the definitive defense because they structurally separate SQL code from data — user input is never interpreted as SQL syntax regardless of content. WAF and parameterized queries are complementary, not alternatives.

q: A company configures Network Firewall rule groups with overlapping rules. Which rule group is evaluated first?
options: The rule group with the lowest priority number
The rule group matching the most specific domain
The rule group created first in the account
All rule groups evaluated in parallel with most restrictive result applied
explain: AWS Network Firewall evaluates rule groups in ascending numerical priority order — a rule group with priority 1 is evaluated before a rule group with priority 100. Within each rule group, rules are evaluated in the order they appear. The first matching rule across all rule groups (in priority order) determines the action. This means critical allow rules for legitimate services should have lower priority numbers than general blocking rules to ensure they are evaluated first.

q: An organization implements stateful Network Firewall rules to detect port scanning. What TCP pattern identifies a port scan?
options: Multiple SYN packets to multiple destination ports on the same host
High packet rates regardless of destination
Unestablished TCP connections — SYN packets without corresponding SYN-ACK responses
All TCP and UDP traffic treated uniformly
explain: A TCP port scan sends SYN packets to many ports but never completes the three-way handshake (no SYN-ACK received, so no ACK sent). AWS Network Firewall stateful rules using Suricata can detect this pattern by tracking connection state: when a source IP generates many SYN packets to closed ports within a time window, each resulting in RST or no response, the pattern is flagged as scanning. This state tracking is impossible with stateless rules, which see only individual packets.

q: A company requires Network Firewall to block all outbound traffic except to a pre-approved list of services. What approach is most efficient to maintain?
options: Individual allow rules for each approved service IP range
Domain list rule group with an allowlist of approved domain names
Deny rules for all unapproved destinations
AWS Managed Rule Groups
explain: Domain list rule groups with ALLOWLIST action are the most efficient way to restrict outbound access to known-good destinations. AWS Network Firewall inspects the SNI (Server Name Indication) in TLS handshakes and the Host header in HTTP requests to identify the domain, then permits only listed domains. This is operationally simpler than maintaining exhaustive IP deny lists (option C) because domain lists don't require updates when service providers change IP addresses.

q: A healthcare provider must enforce least-privilege vendor access. A vendor requests access from the 0.0.0.0/0 range. What is the appropriate response?
